# Transcrição: 05-DPA Product Sets

- **Fonte:** 05-DPA Product Sets
- **Duração:** 08:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

When it comes to the product catalog and creating DPA campaigns, dynamic product ad campaigns, one of the cool things about what Facebook have enabled is the opportunity to create subsets of your product catalog. What I mean by that is, let's say you're running a multi-product store and you've got multiple categories and products and things like that. One of the common things that will occur is you may get products that are recommended that you don't necessarily focus on as your kind of key product. It could be, for example, you're selling products which are ranging in price from $10 up to $150. Maybe it's an apparel store and you've got accessories that are a lot cheaper that you throw into the basket to try and bump up your average order value. You don't necessarily want Facebook to serve those as retargeting ads, your $10, $15, $20 products, because that's going to impact your average order value. What you can do, for example, is create product sets. On the left-hand side here, you can see the product sets. You can create that for any attribute that you can pull out of Facebook. An attribute could be a category name, some kind of naming in the product itself. It could be a variant. It could be price as well. It enables you to create smaller product sets to create your DPA campaigns so that when you're running the retargeting ads, you're telling Facebook to just focus in on these products. Example use case is literally just saying only surface products that are above $30, for example, because below that, you don't really want that to be your focal product in your ad because the return on the ad is just not going to be good enough. That's one of the things that I would consider using. For example, on this store, we can have a look at different attributes. There's condition, brand, price, for example. You can create product sets based on the attributes that you're pulling through. When you create your product set, it could be something you do manually or you use an external tool to pull your data from your e-commerce sales database, Shopify, WooCommerce, Magento, whatever it is, into Facebook. You're probably using the same for Google as well. The data you send is so important because that can have an impact on the product sets that you can create. For example, this is one of our test e-com stores. We were running a Mother's Day promotion. We wanted, for example, for our retargeting ads to just show our Mother's Day collection. What we did here was to literally just select items that were applied as being a Mother's Day product. You can see by the naming convention here, it's quite easily searchable where we can find Mother's Day products.

## [03:00 - 06:04]

Here there's three plus a couple of variants, for example. We've manually added a few others as well. When you create your product set, which you can do in the catalog manager within Business Manager, you can search and add and you can also manually add products into your product set. It could be, and this is an example of a front end product set. You can also create back end product sets related to what they've purchased. This is another thing that is really cool that Facebook have created. For example, if you're selling jeans, for example, at the front end, and that's your main offer, or if you're selling jeans, but you know that generally you've got products that go really well with the jeans, like a jacket, a shirt, or something like that, you can create a product set which only contains jackets, for example, and then target people that bought jeans in your ad set. You can do that as a cross-sell campaign post-purchase. What that means is your front end offer could be the jeans. You could say, you could have a promotion on it, you could have just key messages, whatever it is, even if you break even, even if you make a loss. Let's say, for example, your jeans on average cost $80 and your cost per acquisition is $50, which means considering the cost of goods, you're not really making that much profit. But you know that when people buy the jeans, they become loyal customers and, B, they're likely to purchase again. You could, for example, see that as what we call a loss leader. So it's a key product that you sell at the front of your funnel. And then either through the E-Com funnel on your site, all through retargeting or through email, you're going to get them to buy related products. And the way that would work with Facebook is, for example, you'd create a product set of items that are related to that particular purchase that they've made. So you might have a product set that contains everything but jeans. Could be shirts, could be jackets, socks, shoes, et cetera, to say complete the look. So you could then target people at the bottom of your full funnel system as existing customers and offer them maybe a discount on their repurchase, for example. And especially when they're existing customers, they are more likely to buy again if they're happy and the cost per acquisition is going to be lower as well because you're not prospecting. Now, these people know you, you don't need to take them through a journey of explaining your brand and why it's relevant and things like that. Combining retargeting ads with email flows, with post-purchase funnels to get people to buy more is one of the ways to drive scalability and profitability, not just in E-Com and info products as well. It doesn't matter what it is, but it's that thing about understanding that the first acquisition is your cost per acquisition, but then reacquiring that customer either through ads at low cost or through email and other means will bump up your ultimate, ultimately bump up your profitability, improve your lifetime value and give you far more scope to reinvest

## [06:04 - 08:02]

in your growth as well. So use catalog manager based on your type of products. If you've got a single product store, then catalog manager isn't really going to give you too much. But certainly when you have multiple products going, think about how you can kind of combine product sets. We've created one here for an event. You might do it based on categories, et cetera, et cetera. It's really down to you to define how you do that. And the other thing about the catalog is you can also serve this to your top of funnel ads as well, especially using instant experiences and collection ads. It's a format that I really, really like because it allows you to have your ad copy, have a real nice kind of video as the kind of main impact to get people to stop scrolling and then give them a scrollable list of items as well. So for example, if I was to use this Mother's Day collection top of funnel, I would do my targeting, you know, try and find people that are interested in buying gifts or things for Mother's Day, have a real cool video and some copy as the kind of main portion of that ad and then below pull in my collection of Mother's Day items. So the way that would appear, you know, this is a mobile format is that you would have the big video and the content piece at the top and you have a scrollable list of products below that, which you can see in the link below, in the portion below as an example of how that would be presented. It's a great way of introducing a cold audience to your brand and also getting them to the product fast without them having to go through your page and land on your page, look at products, etc. And it's far cheaper because you're leaving them on Facebook to scroll and engage in your collection, your instant experience ad, which aids lower CPMs and higher profitability for you from the ad as well.
